basic_info.name: Crystalline Polymer Dissolution Temperature
basic_info.name_ja: 結晶性ポリマー溶解温度
basic_info.description: For semicrystalline polymers, dissolution requires both thermodynamic compatibility (low HSP distance) and sufficient thermal energy to overcome crystalline lattice energy. The HSP distance between polymer and solvent, combined with the polymer melting point (Tm), predicts whether dissolution will occur and at what temperature. Solvents with small Ra to the polymer dissolve it at lower temperatures, while poor solvents may only dissolve the polymer above Tm. This framework also predicts LCST and UCST dissolution behavior, where temperature-dependent HSP shifts cause phase separation or dissolution transitions.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Crystalline polymer dissolution is governed by two thermodynamic requirements: (1) the free energy of mixing must be negative (favorable polymer-solvent HSP match, low chi), and (2) the crystal must be destabilized (temperature must approach or exceed the equilibrium dissolution temperature Td). The dissolution temperature Td is depressed below Tm according to the Flory diluent theory: 1/Td - 1/Tm0 = (R/dHu) * (Vu/V1) * (phi_1 - chi * phi_1^2), where dHu is the heat of fusion per repeat unit, Vu is the molar volume of the repeat unit, V1 is the molar volume of the solvent, and phi_1 is the solvent volume fraction. The chi parameter is estimated from HSP distance: chi = Vs * Ra^2 / (6RT). Solvents with lower Ra produce lower chi, which leads to greater Tm depression and lower dissolution temperatures. For LCST behavior, HSP values (particularly dH) decrease with increasing temperature, potentially causing phase separation on heating when hydrogen bonding weakens.
hsp_principle.key_equation: 1/Td - 1/Tm0 = (R/dHu)*(Vu/V1)*(phi1 - chi*phi1^2); chi = Vs*Ra^2/(6*R*T); Ra = sqrt(4*ddD^2 + ddP^2 + ddH^2)
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for potential dissolution; dissolution temperature decreases as RED decreases. RED < 0.5 typically allows dissolution well below Tm.

required_inputs: HSP values (dD, dP, dH) for the crystalline polymer (amorphous phase)
HSP values (dD, dP, dH) for the solvent
Heat of fusion per repeat unit (dHu)
Molar volumes of solvent (V1) and polymer repeat unit (Vu)
data_sources: HSPiP database for polymer and solvent HSP
DSC measurements for Tm and dHu of the specific polymer grade
Literature values for common semicrystalline polymers (PE, PP, PET, nylon, PVDF, etc.)
Temperature-dependent HSP corrections from HSPiP or literature correlations
ATHAS databank for heat capacity and heat of fusion data
complementary_parameters: Polymer molecular weight and molecular weight distribution

limitations: HSP values are typically reported at 25C; temperature dependence corrections are approximate and vary by method
Equilibrium Tm0 is difficult to determine precisely; observed Tm depends on crystallization conditions and heating rate
Flory diluent theory assumes equilibrium thermodynamics; actual dissolution may be kinetically limited by crystal thickness and chain mobility
Chi parameter estimated from HSP is concentration-independent, while real chi often depends on composition
Does not account for solvent-induced crystallization, which can occur when amorphous regions dissolve preferentially
LCST prediction requires accurate temperature-dependent HSP data, which is scarce for many polymer-solvent systems
known_failure_modes: High-density polyethylene in some solvents dissolves at much higher temperatures than predicted due to thick lamellae and high crystallinity
PVDF/DMF system shows LCST behavior driven by strong H-bonding temperature dependence, poorly captured by standard HSP temperature corrections
Polymorphic polymers (e.g., nylon 6 alpha vs. gamma form) dissolve at different temperatures despite identical HSP
Ultra-high molecular weight polymers where dissolution kinetics dominate over thermodynamic predictions
validation_method: Cloud point measurements as function of temperature
Hot-stage optical microscopy observing crystal disappearance

key_references: Flory, P.J. 'Principles of Polymer Chemistry', Cornell University Press, 1953, Chapter 13
Hansen, C.M. 'Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook', 2nd Ed., CRC Press, 2007, Chapter 6
Miller-Chou, B.A.; Koenig, J.L. 'A review of polymer dissolution', Progress in Polymer Science, 2003, 28(8), 1223-1270
Papageorgiou, G.Z.; Bikiaris, D.N. 'Crystallization and melting behavior of three biodegradable poly(alkylene succinates)', Polymer, 2005, 46(26), 12081-12092
Lindvig, T.; Michelsen, M.L.; Kontogeorgis, G.M. 'A Flory-Huggins model based on the Hansen solubility parameters', Fluid Phase Equilibria, 2002, 203(1-2), 247-260
literature.regulatory_relevance: Relevant to pharmaceutical formulation where crystalline API dissolution in polymeric matrices (amorphous solid dispersions) must be controlled; FDA guidance on amorphous solid dispersions references thermodynamic miscibility screening [uncertain]

industry_sectors: Textile/fiber industry (solution spinning of crystalline polymers like UHMWPE, PAN)
Pharmaceutical (amorphous solid dispersion design for poorly soluble crystalline APIs)
Membrane manufacturing (PVDF, cellulose acetate membrane casting from solution)
Recycling (selective dissolution of crystalline plastics for chemical recycling)
Polymer processing (solvent welding and bonding of semicrystalline parts)
commercial_tools: Aspen Plus / Aspen Polymers (Flory-Huggins with T-dependent chi)
DETHERM database (thermophysical property data for validation)
